figures.caption: Table 1: Closed-form VQA prompt sensitivity results on pneumonia detection. Corr = correct, Inc = incorrect, Ref = refused.
Table 5: GPT-5.1 evaluator prompt variants used in the description-based pipeline. Each prompt is prepended to the VLM-generated description. All variants require a binary Yes/No response.

Table 6: Average performance across prompts for the without answer in one word setting. Values are reported as mean ± standard deviation across prompts. Corr = correct, Inc = incorrect, Ref = refused.

Figure 1: Overview of our four tasks. Visually salient tasks (e.g., brain tumor) are reliably solved, while more complex tasks (e.g., pneumonia, skin cancer, Invasive Ductal Carcinoma (IDC)) exhibit instability or near-random performance.
Figure 2: Small prompt changes cause inconsistent decisions in both medical and general VLMs.
Table 2: Average performance across all diseases (brain tumor, pneumonia, IDC, and skin cancer), comparing one-word and unconstrained VQA.
Table 11: Evaluation results for IDC without a one-word answer requirement. Corr = correct, Inc = incorrect, Ref = refused. ↑ indicates higher is better; ↓ indicates lower is better.
Table 12: Evaluation results for IDC with a one-word answer requirement. Corr = correct, Inc = incorrect, Ref = refused. ↑ indicates higher is better; ↓ indicates lower is better.
Table 3: Results from description-based evaluation, where GPT-5.1 diagnoses diseases using VLM-generated descriptions.
Table 13: Evaluation results for Skin Cancer without a one-word answer requirement. Corr = correct, Inc = incorrect, Ref = refused. ↑ indicates higher is better; ↓ indicates lower is better.
Table 14: Evaluation results for Skin Cancer with a one-word answer requirement. Corr = correct, Inc = incorrect, Ref = refused. ↑ indicates higher is better; ↓ indicates lower is better.
Table 9: Evaluation results for Brain Tumor with a one-word answer requirement. Corr = correct, Inc = incorrect, Ref = refused. ↑ indicates higher is better; ↓ indicates lower is better.
Table 10: Evaluation results for Pneumonia without a one-word answer requirement. Corr = correct, Inc = incorrect, Ref = refused. ↑ indicates higher is better; ↓ indicates lower is better.
Table 4: Prompts used for each disease classification task.

Figure 4: t-SNE projections of vision encoder embeddings of the four datasets. Each point represents an image embedding, colored by ground-truth label. Silhouette scores are reported in each subplot to quantify class separability.

Figure 3: Performance (F1all) across disease classification tasks for base and medically fine-tuned models, averaged across all prompt variants. Medical fine-tuning yields improvements on some tasks but does not consistently outperform base models.

Table 7: Average performance across prompts for the with answer in one word setting. Values are reported as mean with standard deviation in smaller font. Corr = correct, Inc = incorrect, Ref = refused.
Table 8: Evaluation results for Brain Tumor without a one-word answer requirement. Corr = correct, Inc = incorrect, Ref = refused. ↑ indicates higher is better; ↓ indicates lower is better.
